coredata.dc:title: Phyllanthus emblica seed extract mediated synthesis of PdNPs against antibacterial, heamolytic and cytotoxic studies
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology
coredata.dc:creator: Dinesh, Murugesan
Roopan, Selvaraj Mohana
Selvaraj, Chinnadurai Immanuel
Arunachalam, Prabhakarn
coredata.dc:description: Ecofriendly synthesis of Palladium nanoparticles (PdNPs) were achieved using Phyllanthus emblica (P. emblica) seeds as reducing agent. Further the ecofriendly synthesized PdNPs were subjected for various analytical techniques like UV–Vis, FT-IR, XRD, Zeta potential, SEM and TEM. The results indicated that green synthesized PdNPs were spherical in shape with average particle size of 28±2nm with moderate stability. Further the synthesized PdNPs and extract were subjected for its antibacterial studies against various disease causing pathogens by agar well diffusion method. Seed extract resulted in 8.9±1.46mm against B. subtilis and PdNPs showed 9.6±1.10mm against S. aureus and synthesized PdNPs and extract were tested for hemolytic which resulted in 20% and 10% respectively. Toxicity studies were done against Artemia salina (A. salina). The LC50 value of green synthesized P. emblica capped PdNPs and the P. emblica seed extract were found to be less toxic for A. salina with a value of 1.00μg/mL and 1.25μg/mL. In addition samples were checked for in vitro cytotoxicity assays on HeLa cell lines.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phyllanthus emblica
PdNPs
Haemolysis
Artemia salina
HeLa cell lines

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Green Synthesis of P. emblica Capped PdNPs
Characterization of P. emblica Capped PdNPs
Assessment of Toxicity Testing in Artemia salina
UV–Visible Spectra of P. emblica Capped PdNPs
XRD Studies of P. emblica Capped PdNPs
FT-IR Analysis of P. emblica Capped PdNPs
Morphological Studies and Zeta Potential of P. emblica Capped PdNPs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern of P. emblica capped PdNPs.
FT-IR spectra of P. emblica seed extract, palladium acetate (Pd(OAc)2) and PdNPs solution.

(a) Scanning electron Microscopic image of PdNPs capped by P. emblica seed extract; (b) Transmission electron Microscopic image of PdNPs capped by P. emblica seed extract; Zeta potential for the synthesized PdNPs indicating the value of −39.9mV.

(a) Antibacterial activity of P. emblica seed extract against pathogenic microorganisms compared with a positive control, streptomycin indicated by zone of inhibition studies.; (b) Antibacterial activity of P. emblica capped nanoparticle against pathogenic microorganisms compared with a positive control, streptomycin indicated by zone of inhibition studies.
Haemolytic activity (in percentage) for P. emblica seed extract and P. emblica capped PdNPs.

(a) Toxicity studies using P. emblica seed extract and A. salina indicating the LC50 value and mortality rate; (b) Toxicity studies using P. emblica seed extract and A. salina indicating the LC50 value and mortality rate.
Cytotoxic effect of P. emblica seed extract and PdNPs against HeLa cell lines.
Zone of inhibition for P. emblica seed extract for the tested bacterial species with streptomycin as positive control.
Zone of inhibition for PdNPs for the tested bacterial species with streptomycin as positive control.
Haemolysis percentage of the P. emblica seed extract, PdNPS and positive control (Triton X-100).
Cytotoxicity assay with HeLa cells using P. emblica seed extract and PdNPs, positive control used was HeLa cell lines.

head.ce:title: Phyllanthus emblica seed extract mediated synthesis of PdNPs against antibacterial, heamolytic and cytotoxic studies

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology, School of Biosciences and Technology, VIT University, Vellore 632 014, Tamilnadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry of Heterocycles & Natural Product Research Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, School of Advanced Sciences, VIT University, Vellore 632 014, Tamilnadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chemistry of Heterocycles & Natural Product Research Laboratory

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electrochemistry Research Group, Chemistry Department, College of Science, King Saud University, Riyadh 11451, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ecofriendly synthesis of Palladium nanoparticles (PdNPs) were achieved using Phyllanthus emblica (P. emblica) seeds as reducing agent. Further the ecofriendly synthesized PdNPs were subjected for various analytical techniques like UV–Vis, FT-IR, XRD, Zeta potential, SEM and TEM. The results indicated that green synthesized PdNPs were spherical in shape with average particle size of 28±2nm with moderate stability. Further the synthesized PdNPs and extract were subjected for its antibacterial studies against various disease causing pathogens by agar well diffusion method. Seed extract resulted in 8.9±1.46mm against B. subtilis and PdNPs showed 9.6±1.10mm against S. aureus and synthesized PdNPs and extract were tested for hemolytic which resulted in 20% and 10% respectively. Toxicity studies were done against Artemia salina (A. salina). The LC50 value of green synthesized P. emblica capped PdNPs and the P. emblica seed extract were found to be less toxic for A. salina with a value of 1.00μg/mL and 1.25μg/mL. In addition samples were checked for in vitro cytotoxicity assays on HeLa cell lines.

ce:section.ce:para: In the last decade, nanoparticles plays a versatile and valuable role in many fields because of its interdisciplinary approach [1]. The transition metal nanoparticles are focused according to recent technology due to their high potency. Mostly biosynthetic methods of nanoparticle have been considered as preferable one, when compared to physiochemical methods [2]. Due to the eco-friendly and non-toxicity nature to the environment, our interest focused on biological approach of synthesis. Recently many scientists have been focused towards biosynthetic approach for the production of Ag, Pd, Au, Pb, etc., nanoparticles [3]. The utilization of plant mediated nanoparticles is more advantageous over microorganisms, since the latter involves in maintaining the microbial culture in an aseptic conditions [4]. The physiochemical methods used often are toxic, costly and non-eco-friendly [5]. Hence the researchers focused their method of synthesis towards the bio-reduction using fungi, plants and microorganism for the greater stability of nanoparticles which are non-toxic and also utilized as organic catalysts [6–16]. Most of the researchers focused on several plants and plant parts for synthesis of nanoparticles and utilized for several applicational studies [17–31].

The PdNPs are most probably used in dehydrogenation reaction, CH olefination and Suzuki cross coupling reaction [32]. Recently PdNPs were used in diagnostic applications as they get bound to single stranded DNA without damaging their structure [33]. Biosensor for glucose estimation can be done when the palladium nanoparticles are doped to chitosan-graphene [34]. Palladium nanoparticles can be synthesized from the extracts of coffee, tea and custard apple peels, as they were capable to act as reducing agents at room temperature. The extracts of different plants may contain different secondary metabolites for conversion of metal starting material to metal nanoparticles [35]. Modified electrodes of PdNPs with multi-walled carbon nanotubes were utilized for electro catalytic oxidation [36]. Different catalyst systems were examined for ammonia borane hydrolysis, among which palladium-rhodium is an efficient catalyst for the hydrogen generation [37]. Palladium is used to detect hydrogen as a sensor [38]. The main advantage of palladium is high affinity to the hydrogen adsorption [39]. In the sensor application different methods has been adapted to synthesize PdNPs.

The impact of co-poisoning can be reduced significantly and enhances the activity by the help of palladium catalyst [40]. According to colloidal spray deposition one of the fabrication methods used for palladium nanoparticles to achieve the size of the nanoparticles that ranges between 100 and 300nm [41]. Palladium was mostly used in industrial applications such as catalytic and reduction of automobile pollutants [42].

In Phyllanthacea; P. emblica is one of the major fruits used for medical purposes [43]. The P. emblica plant species were widely present in Asian regions. The P. emblica fruit is mostly utilized in the field of Unani and ayurvedic [44]. Fruit has the nature of cooling capacity, when the body suffers from heat; it has high content of Vitamin C [45–47].

In this study, antibacterial activity has been tested for both seed extract and the synthesized P. emblica capped PdNPs by well diffusion method. The in vitro toxicity has been tested in blood by haemolytic activity [48]. To know the level of toxicity of the synthesized P. emblica capped PdNPs and the methanolic seed extract, toxicological studies have been done against brine shrimp (Artemia salina). Artemia salina is one of the aquatic organisms mostly used for toxicological studies nowadays. A. salina is more versatile and capable to adapt any kind of environmental conditions [49]. Cytotoxicity was assessed by MTT assay and further evaluated against the HeLa cell lines [50].

Keeping the above points in mind, the focus of this study is to synthesize non-toxic P. emblica capped PdNPs using P. emblica seeds extract and to evaluate their potential biological applications such as toxicity studies which includes haemolytic activity, toxicity towards A. salina and cytotoxicity assay against HeLa cell lines and the antibacterial activity.

The fruits of P. emblica were collected from in and around at state of Tamilnadu, Vellore City, Ambur town and its co-ordinates are (12.78°N 78.7°E). Further by the help of botanist Dr. P. Jayaraman we authenticated the fruit and authenticated number is PARC/2014/2058. Palladium acetate was purchased from Sigma Aldrich Chemicals Ltd., Mumbai, India. Methanol (Spectrochem research laboratories, Mumbai, India), Brine shrimp cysts (Artemia salina) were procured and cultured under laboratory conditions.

Phyllanthus emblica fruits were collected and the seeds were separated from the fruit. The seeds were washed in running water, then in excess distilled water and shade dried for a week. After drying, the seeds were chopped and grounded by Deluxe Prestige high speed mechanical blender (Chennai, India). The seed powder was used for further studies. Initially, to remove hydrocarbons the dried seed powder was socked in petroleum ether and then extracted with methanol by immersion method. Further the solvent were distilled and utilized for further purpose (Fig. SI 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green Synthesis of P. emblica Capped PdNPs
ce:section.ce:para: P. emblica capped PdNPs were prepared by using conventional method. One hundred mL of 1M palladium acetate (Pd(OAc)2) solution was prepared in sterile distilled water. The final solution was prepared by the mixture of 80mL of Pd(OAc)2 and 20mL of P. emblica methanol extract with a final concentration of 0.87M Pd(OAc)2 in the solution. The reaction mixer was stirred at 60°C for 3h. UV–Visible spectroscopy results were observed at every 30min time interval. After the visual indication of colour change in the solution, it was centrifuged at 3000ppm for 20min. The same procedure was repeated thrice for the settlement of P. emblica PdNPs. The P. emblica capped PdNPs pellets were separated and dried at 400°C in furnace (Roopan et al. 2013).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of P. emblica Capped PdNPs

ce:section.ce:para: The PdNPs were processed between the wavelength of 200–800nm by using Shimadzu UVd-1800 PC, Japan. Further PdNPs absorbance were observed (Roopan et al., 2015).

XRD analysis were processed by Advance Powder X-ray diffractometer D8, (Bruker, Germany) taken from 10 to 80°. The particles size can be calculated by Scherrer formula,
D = Kλ / β cos θ
D =particle size, K =Scherer constant (0.94)λ =wavelength, β =Half Width Full Maximum, θ =diffraction angle.
FTIR analysis was performed using (Alpha T, Bruker, Germany) to identify the functional groups from the wavenumber of 400 to 4000cm−1.

The synthesized nanoparticle size analyzed using Hitachi H-7100 SEM voltage of120kV, with the parameters to be set as EHT 19.79kV, Extractor V 4.40kV, Extractor I 157μA, Fil I 2.410A, Fil I Target 2.410A, Extractor A Target 4.40A, EHT Target 19.79kV. Samples were submitted in powder form at room temperature.
The palladium nanoparticles samples were dispersed in water under ultrasonic treatment and then dropped on the carbon-coated copper grids (JEM-1230, JEOL, USA) at 80kV.
To identify the stability of the synthesized PdNPs we subjected to Horiba Nano particle analyzer.

In order to study antibacterial activity for the seed extract and PdNPs against four pathogens namely P.aeruginosa, B. subtilis, S. aureus and P. mirabilis was assessed by agar well diffusion method. Standard antibiotic streptomycin was used at the concentration of (50μg/mL) and kept as a stock solution. Sterile distilled water was utilized as negative control. The medium (Muller Hinton agar) was autoclaved and poured in the Petri plates and allowed to solidify. The pure cultures were spread over the Petri plates by cotton swab. The wells were made by a sterile well puncher. The P. emblica seed extract and the PdNPs were prepared at three different concentrations 50, 75 and 100mg/mL respectively by diluting it with sterile distilled water; in each well the prepared samples were added. The inoculated plates were incubated for 24h at 37°C. The incubated plates were then removed and examined for the zone of inhibition (mm) [5]. Triplicates were performed for the tests. In addition, Minimal Inhibitory Concentration (MIC) was performed for the samples by serial dilution method using nutrient broth in sterile test tubes. The samples were prepared at the range from 10 to 500μg/mL. In each tube 100μL of bacterial suspension were added and incubated overnight at 37°C. The uncultured broths in test tubes were used as a negative control.

The prepared P. emblica capped PdNPs and the extracts were evaluated for the haemolytic activity. According to [51], 4mL of blood sample was collected from a healthy volunteer (B+ve) aged 23years old, in the sterile falcon tube with 5.00mg of EDTA. In 1000 RPM, at 4°C for 10min blood was centrifuged and plasma was separated. Further with the help of 1× PBS, pH7.4 the erythrocytes were washed for 5min. This process were repeated thrice and then centrifuged for three times at 5min interval. The pellets formed were separated and diluted in sterile 0.9% NaCl solution, later on in sterile D-PBS [Dulbecco's phosphate buffer saline, 1/24 (v/v)] maintained at pH7.00, containing 0.5mM boric acid and 1mM calcium chloride. The 96 well plates were taken and in each well 0.85% NaCl of 100μL containing solution of 10mM CaCl2 was added, along with 100μL of P. emblica extract was added in each well at different concentrations (250, 500, 750 and 1000μg/mL) and finally 100μL of diluted blood in DPBS were added to well. One hundred microlitres of normal saline is taken as negative control and whereas 0.1% Triton X-100 was taken as a positive control. Then incubated for 30min in room temperature and further taken for centrifuge. Finally we removed the supernatant and precipitated HB were monitored at 630nm. The percentage of haemolysis was calculated by the formula,
Percentage of lysis = Test sample / positive control × 100 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of Toxicity Testing in Artemia salina
ce:section.ce:para:
The aquatic organism A. salina was grown in sea water and the toxicity tests were carried out in laboratory conditions. Stock solution was prepared by mixing 10μg/mL of PdNPs and P. emblica seed extract with 19mL of sea water separately. By using six well plates, various concentrations of P. emblica seed extract and P. emblica capped PdNPs were tested against A. salina for 14 different concentrations and the experiment was performed in three replicates. Ten Naupliis' were added in each well; with sea water as a positive control and kept it for 24h incubation in room temperature. The movement of each nauplius was observed after 24h. The mortality rate was checked, compared and correlated with control, from the obtained data; the results were calculated using the following formula,
Percentage of Mortality =
A M
/ P
T
× 100
where, AM =Number of Dead A. salina Naupliis and PT =Total number of population.

Lethal Count (LC50) value was calculated according to the percentage of mortality in each well at different concentrations and the graph was plotted. The result was calculated for both P. emblica capped PdNPs and P. emblica methanol seed extract. The best fit method was followed in this toxicity assay [24].

The basic principle of MTT assay is the reduction of yellow tetrazolium dye to a purple formazan product by the mitochondria in living cells. DMEM (Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium) with Fetal Bovine Serum (10%) in 5% CO2 at 37°C was used to maintained the living cells. Approximately 1.2×104 cells/well were fixed to 96 tissue culture plate and allowed to fix in the well at 37°C. Samples of various concentrations (50, 100 and 150μg/100μL) were incubated for 24h in the well, after discarding the medium from it. After completion of the process fresh medium (100μL) and 5mg/mL MTT (10μL) was added to the wells. The existing medium was removed from the plate after 4h and fresh DMSO (100μL) was added to 96 tissue culture plate to dissolve the formazan crystal. The absorbance of the resulting solution was measured at 570nm by using microtitre plate reader. Cyclophosphamide was used as positive control and IC50 value is calculated [25].

The survival of the cells was calculated by
Viability
%
=
OD Test
/ OD
Control
× 100
Cytotoxicity
%
= 100 − Viability percentage

The Data were analyzed statistically using GraphPad Prism version 6.00 for Windows, GraphPad Software, La Jolla California, USA. All tests were performed in triplicates. P <0.05 is considered to be statistically significantly. Data were expressed as mean±SD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–Visible Spectra of P. emblica Capped PdNPs
ce:section.ce:para: Conversions of palladium acetate to P. emblica capped PdNPs were perceived using UV–Visible spectra at different intervals of time. Change of colour from light brown to dark brown indicates the conversion of Pd (II) ions to P. emblica capped PdNPs. With the effect of time the absorbance were recorded at 200–800nm. The clear peak in surface Plasmon resonance was 258nm, in the time interval of 90min (Fig. SI 2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: XRD Studies of P. emblica Capped PdNPs
ce:section.ce:para: To characterize the crystalline pattern of P. emblica capped PdNPs XRD analysis were performed and the crystalline structure was shown in Fig. 1 , further correlated with JCPDS (Joint Committee on Powder Diffraction Standards) and the resulted number was 89-4897. By this data it shows that the P. emblica capped PdNPs formed were crystalline in nature. According to Full Width Half Maximum the crystalline peak has been measured clearly, from the Scherer formula the average size of the particle was 28±2nm. Occurrence of the *** indicates due to secondary metabolites presence which is present in P. emblica extract.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FT-IR Analysis of P. emblica Capped PdNPs
ce:section.ce:para: The FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2 ) for P. emblica seed extract, Pd(OAc)2 and PdNPs clearly confirms the formation of nanoparticles. An observed broader band at 3256cm−1for –OH functionalized moieties indicated the presence of polyhydroxy compounds in P. emblica seed extract; and a sharp peak was also noticed at 1719cm−1 because of the presence of carbonyl (CO) group, the intensity peak in the range of 1719cm−1, cleaves into two peaks which responsible for the reduction of Pd(OAc)2 to PdNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological Studies and Zeta Potential of P. emblica Capped PdNPs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized P. emblica capped PdNPs were further analyzed to identify its morphological structure. The morphological studies were performed using Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) indicated that the prepared P. emblica capped PdNPs were spherical in shape (Fig. 3a). P. emblica capped PdNPs were evaluated using transmission electron microscope (TEM) The size of the particles observed were around 30nm (Fig. 3b). Green synthesized P. emblica capped PdNPs were further subjected for its stability using Zeta potential. From the result it is concluded that the prepared nanoparticles have moderate stability of −39.9mV (Fig. 3c).

Antibacterial activity has been tested for four different pathogens against P. emblica methanolic seed extract and PdNPs. Agar-well diffusion method was followed for the activity. The zones of inhibition were measured to test the effectiveness of the extract and PdNPs against the bacterial pathogens. Seed extract showed maximum zone of inhibition against B. subtilis (8.9±1.46mm) followed by S. aureus (8.2±1.10mm) at 100mg/mL and PdNPs showed maximum zone of inhibition against S. aureus (9.6±1.10mm) at 100mg/mL and (8.5±1.21mm) at 75mg/mL followed by P. aeruginosa (7.6±0.79mm) at 100mg/mL. The positive control streptomycin showed maximum zone of inhibition (11.6±1.05mm) at 75μg/mL. The remaining zones were represented in Tables 1a and 1b
and the graph has been plotted and shown in (Fig. 4a and b).

Minimum inhibitory concentration studies (MIC) for the seed extract and PdNPs were done against the pathogens (Table 2 ). MIC studies indicated B. subtilis was inhibited at lower concentration (29±7μg/mL) and (27±1μg/mL) for the extract and PdNPs, respectively. P. mirabilis was the most resistant pathogen among the other organisms compared in this study.

To determine the toxicity testing of bioactive compounds simple assay has been performed using Artemia salina Nauplii. After 24h incubation in room temperature the mortality rate was calculated using the magnifying lens. The LC50 value is calculated from the average of three replicates and a graph was plotted using Best Fit-line method, for seed extract the LC50 value is 1.00μg/mL for PdNPs the LC50 value is 1.25μg/mL, shown (Fig. 6a, b).

The aim of the current study is to prepare PdNPs from the seeds of P. emblica. The PdNPs were synthesized and characterized using various analytic techniques. The change of colour from light brown to dark brown indicates formation of PdNPs after 2h and also confirmed in UV–Vis spectrometer the absorbance range found at 250–290nm. From XRD the PdNPs formation has been confirmed by the peaks are crystalline in nature. The nanoparticles are around 28±2nm calculated using Scherer's formula. In FT-IR on addition of methanolic seed extract to Pd(OAc)2 the reduction of (electron donation group) palladium (II) ions to palladium (0) ions by the help of the functional groups present in the P. emblica seed extract. The morphology studies have been carried out using Electron Microscopy, in which the nanoparticles are nano form and spherical in shape and the size was around 30nm. The stability was calculated using zeta potential shows −39.9mV which shows synthesized PdNPs were moderate stability. Microorganisms have genetic capability to transmit and acquire resistance to the extracts and PdNPs. In order to determine the resistance four pathogens were tested. P. mirabilis was the most resistant pathogen among the other organisms compared in this study. To check the toxicity of both seed extract and PdNPs, Haemolytic assay has been performed, when the drug is injected into human body it does not cause any lysis to blood and should not damage RBCs. If it damages the RBCs then it is toxic to humans. The liberated RBCs were estimated to check the blood lysis, the seed extract lysis 8.55% and PdNPs lysis up to 9.69%. Then toxicity was tested against A. salina one of aquatic organism, the LC50 has been calculated for seed extract the LC50 value is 1.00μg/mL and for PdNPs the LC50 value is 1.25μg/mL. Finally tested against HeLa cell lines the IC50 value has been calculated for seed extract it was 30.45μg/mL and PdNPs it was 15.15μg/mL. From all the toxicity test it shows seed extract and PdNPs were nontoxic can be used in minor concentration.

Ecofriendly synthesis of PdNPs was achieved using P. emblica methanol seed which resulted in spherical shape with average size of 28±2nm. Antibacterial studies of PdNPs were resulted significantly against B. subtilis and S. aureus. Hemolytic assay and toxicity testing were resulted to be less toxic and doe dependent reaction. Furthermore P. emblica seed extract has the potential for cell death against human cervical cancer cells.
The authors confirm that there are no conflicts of interest associated with this publication.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The corresponding authors are thankful to the management of VIT University for their research support. Prabhakarn Arunachalam extend their gratitude to the Deanship of Scientific Research, College of Science Research Centre, King Saud University, Saudi Arabia.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotobiol.2016.12.012.

Hydrogen transport properties of palladium film prepared by colloidal spray deposition
Effect of pre-treatments on quality of solar dried amla
A study on the degradation kinetics of visual green colour in spinach (Spinacea oleracea L.) and the effect of salt therein
Free radicals, oxidative stress, and antioxidants in human health and disease
Antioxidant capacity of polyphenolic extracts from leaves of Crataegus laevigata and Crataegus monogyna (hawthorn) subjected to drought and cold stress
A full-length protocol to test hemolytic activity of palytoxin on human erythrocytes
Toxicity effect of silver nanoparticles in brine shrimp Artemia
Rapid colorimetric assay for cellular growth and survival: application to proliferation and cytotoxicity assays
Inhibitory potential of Turbinaria ornata against key metabolic enzymes linked to diabetes
